narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 77% chance that you would not pass based on your academic and background details. This high predicted risk of failure automatically triggers a rejection to ensure the program's overall success rates. The model's decision hinged most strongly on your Year 3 school decile ranking, which placed you in the bottom group and significantly pushed the prediction toward expecting a bar failure, unlike the much higher mid-level deciles typical of students who passed. Next in influence was your LSAT score of 24, well below the average of 37 for bar passers, further tilting the odds against passing in the model's view. Your Year 1 decile of 1 also contributed notably in the same direction, signaling early struggles compared to the top-half deciles common among successful students. These three academic indicators stood out as the primary drivers, outweighing other factors like your undergraduate GPA of 2.90 or your low family income bracket, which is rare among bar passers who mostly come from middle to upper-middle income families. While being full-time is a positive match for most who pass, it wasn't enough to offset these core weaknesses. Compared to typical admitted students, your profile shows consistently lower performance metrics that the model links strongly to bar exam challenges. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—it's a data-driven call to protect the school's outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to 37 or higher, strengthening your early and later undergrad deciles through additional coursework or programs, and exploring resources tailored for students from similar income situations to build a stronger case next time. With targeted improvements here, you could shift your predicted odds dramatically in future applications. Many students in tough spots have turned things around by focusing on these exact areas. Keep pushing—you've got this.
